m1-overview.body:
Welcome to the first block of Module 1. By the end of it you'll name the four scales at which a new technology can disrupt — Product, Bundle, Industry, System — and you'll have Edison's bulb to hang each of them on.

Learning outcomes

  • Distinguish disruption of a Product, a Bundle, an Industry, and a System — and place historical and current cases at each scale.
  • Identify the "killer app" inside a broader system disruption and explain why it sets the entry point.
  • Recognise that a system disruption is rarely about one technology; it is about what becomes possible after the entry technology lands.

m1-lesson-1.videoTitle: Lesson · Edison's quiet certainty — and four scales of disruption
m1-lesson-1.body:
October 1879. Menlo Park, New Jersey. Edison and his team have been at this for years. Cotton. Platinum. Cedar wood. Hair from a man's beard. Thousands of filaments tried, thousands burned out — sometimes after seconds, sometimes after minutes, never long enough to matter. Tonight they're watching a thin strip of carbonised bamboo glow. Ten hours pass. Twenty. Forty. The thread doesn't break.

What I want you to notice is not the celebration. There isn't any, not really. There is a quiet certainty in the room. Edison understands, in that hour, that gas lamps are finished. He cannot quite see the full shape of what is coming next, but he can already see far enough: the bulb in his hand will render an entire industry obsolete, will reshape what is possible inside cities and factories and homes, will change the rhythm of daily life itself.

That moment is useful because it lets us name something the framework needs. Not all disruption is the same size. Sometimes a new technology only replaces an old technology, one for one. Sometimes it pulls apart a bundle of services. Sometimes it kills an industry. And sometimes — rarely — it remakes an entire system.

Bradd's frame · the four scales of disruption

Product → Bundle → Industry → System

Product. Tungsten replaces bamboo in the bulb. Same product, better materials.

Bundle. Electric light replaces the lighting strand of gas service — but gas keeps cooking.

Industry. Electricity then disrupts coal gas, kerosene, the whole gas-utility industry.

System. Electrification rewires daily life: electric motors, refrigeration, radio, TV, computers, the internet, the 24-hour city. The bulb was the entry point.

The reason these four scales matter is forecasting. If you have correctly identified a disruption at the Industry scale and you are forecasting it as if it were a Product swap, your numbers are about to be very wrong. And the more painful failure mode — the one that destroys firms with strong incumbents — is identifying a System disruption but only modelling its first-order effects. You can be right about the entry technology and still completely miss what becomes possible after it lands.

From Bradd · the Kodak mis-classification

Kodak invented the digital camera in 1975 — an engineer named Steven Sasson, on the company's payroll, built the first one. Kodak read the technology correctly at the Product layer (a new image sensor was going to replace film) and even at the Industry layer (the film-processing industry was going to die). But they classified it as Industry, not System. They missed the cascade: digital sensors → smartphones → networked image sharing → social platforms → image as the primary unit of communication. Kodak prepared for an industry transition. What arrived was a system flip. They went bankrupt in 2012.

The killer-app concept is the second move worth catching here. The bulb was not the point. The bulb was the killer app — the application sufficient on its own to justify wiring households, after which everything downstream could ride in on the same wire. No one in 1879 was wiring up their house for the refrigerator. They were wiring up for the bulb. Once the wire was in, the refrigerator, the radio, the television, the computer, the internet, the smartphone — all of them rode in on it.

Bradd's frame · the killer-app waterfall

One justified investment unlocks everything downstream

Once households said yes to one application, the marginal cost of saying yes to the next one was almost zero. The bulb justified the wire. The wire then carried everything that came after.

Bradd's standing recommendation for any analysis: name the killer app first, then name the cascade it unlocks. If you cannot name the cascade in concrete terms, you have not yet found a System disruption — you have found an Industry one and you are projecting. That is fine. Industry disruptions matter. They just don't reshape civilisation, and the strategic stakes of confusing the two are enormous.

The most useful application of this frame to the present: the convergence of solar, wind, and batteries (SWB) is currently being read by most analysts as an Industry disruption (the electricity-generation industry shifts from fossil-fuel boilers to SWB hardware). The STDF reads it as a System disruption — where SWB is the bulb, and the cascade is everything we will build once electricity is structurally too cheap to meter. We will return to this case in Module 3. For now, just notice that the same evidence supports two completely different forecasts depending on which scale you classify it at.

?
Bradd · mid-lesson check. Pause. Take one technology change you have lived through (the smartphone, the EV, LED bulbs, anything). Place it at one of the four scales. If your gut answer was "Product," ask whether you have under-classified it. If your gut answer was "System," ask whether you have over-classified it. The interesting cases are the ones that have moved between scales over time — and the most expensive cases are the ones, like Kodak, where the firm correctly read three of the four scales and got the fourth one wrong.
The killer-app technology gets all the attention, and the system disruption that follows it gets named, in retrospect, as if everyone saw it coming. Almost no one saw it coming.

Next: do the reading. Then the mentor session — that is where we apply the four scales to a case you bring, live.

m1-reading.title: Libby, Pattern of Progress, Chapter 1 (excerpt)
m1-reading.body:
A short excerpt from Chapter 1 of The Pattern of Progress. Read for the argument, not the case detail.

The chapter walks the four scales explicitly. The move worth catching is the one between "industry disruption" and "system disruption" — Bradd is precise about why those are not the same thing. Industries die regularly. Systems flip rarely.

Read with these questions in hand

  • Name one historical case at each of the four scales (use ones the lesson didn't).
  • What test would you apply to a current case to decide between Industry- and System-scale disruption?
  • What is the analogous killer app for SWB superpower in Bradd's account?

m1-mentor.body: A mentor session with Bradd on applying the four scales to a case you bring.

chat.text: Bring me a technology change you have lived through. Doesn't have to be the most important one. The one you have first-hand experience with works best.
Streaming video. I cancelled cable in 2018 and never went back.
Good case. Product layer — what got replaced one-for-one?
The cable set-top box. A streaming app on a smart TV does the same job.
Bundle — what strand got pulled out of the cable bundle?
On-demand first. Sports came later.
Industry?
Cable as a content-distribution industry. The cable companies survived as ISPs but the distribution business collapsed.
System — this is where most analysts stop short. What changed about daily life that was not predicted by 'people will watch TV via the internet'?
Binge-watching. Prestige TV. Algorithmic recommendation as the cultural gatekeeper. The collapse of appointment viewing — including the news.
Good. Now name the killer app. Which application was sufficient on its own to justify the infrastructure?
Netflix on-demand. Specifically House of Cards in 2013 — original content people would only get if they signed up. That justified the subscription.
Right. And once subscriptions were in, what rode in on the same wire?

Original-content arms race across every streamer. Then TikTok and YouTube turning everyone into a producer. Then live sports moving to streaming. Then the news collapsing because appointment viewing died.
That's a clean cascade. Now the move that makes this analysis useful for a current decision. If you ran a regional broadcaster in 2026, what would the four-scales analysis tell you to stop doing?

Stop investing in cable affiliate distribution — that's Industry-dead. Stop optimising for prime-time appointment viewing — that's System-dead. Start investing in cumulative on-demand catalogue and one strong original-content franchise that justifies a subscription. Different business shape entirely.

Exactly. That's the move that turns this framework from a classification exercise into a strategic one. Save this conversation — most of your Block 1 essay is in it. And tag what you just said about the regional broadcaster for the Module 1 capstone — it is structurally the same move we will make on humanoid robots, on SWB, on drones.

questions.q: Tungsten replacing bamboo as the filament material in the light bulb is best classified as:
options: A Product-scale disruption — same bulb, better materials
A Bundle-scale disruption — tungsten unbundles incandescent from fluorescent
An Industry-scale disruption — the carbon-filament industry is destroyed
A System-scale disruption — daily life changes around the new bulb
questions.feedback: Correct. Same product, better materials. The smallest scale of disruption — and the easiest to forecast.

questions.q: Bradd's distinction between an Industry disruption and a System disruption is:
options: Industries die when their product is replaced; systems flip when daily life is rewired around what the entry technology made possible
Industry disruption is faster than system disruption
System disruption only happens with energy technologies
The two are synonyms
questions.feedback: Correct. The test is whether the new technology kills its incumbent industry only, or whether it unlocks a cascade of further technologies that rewire how life is lived.

questions.q: The 'killer app' concept in Bradd's chapter names:
options: The most useful application of a technology that ever existed
The entry technology that justifies adoption on its own, opening a corridor for everything downstream
The application that destroys the most incumbent jobs
The most expensive application of a technology
questions.feedback: Correct. The killer app clears the adoption bar on its own; downstream applications then ride in on the same infrastructure.

questions.q: A pure-EV company displaces ICE vehicles but private ownership / parking / car-shaped suburbs remain. This reads as:
options: System — cars have changed
Product only — same vehicle, different drivetrain
Industry disruption that may or may not become System depending on whether TaaS follows
Not a disruption
questions.feedback: Correct. The EV alone is an industry disruption. The system disruption arrives only if autonomous + fleet ownership + ride-hailing converge into TaaS. That cascade is Module 4.

m1-discussion.prompt: Pick a technology change you have personally lived through. Place it at one of the four scales (Product / Bundle / Industry / System) and defend the placement in two paragraphs. Then ask one classmate to argue you've under-classified or over-classified it.

posts.name: Jamie Arbib
posts.body: WhatsApp displacing SMS reads as a Product swap to most people. But once it became free at scale it killed the SMS-revenue line of every mobile operator — that's Industry. And once group chats became the default coordination layer for families, businesses, and protests, it changed political organising in countries with weak media institutions. That last bit is System. Most analysis stops at Product.

posts.name: Bradd Libby
posts.body: Good case. Note the system layer is where most of WhatsApp's geopolitical consequences sit. Same pattern with drones in Block 5.

posts.name: Anastasia Bazir
posts.body: Counter-example: smart speakers. Read as System by everyone in 2017. Turned out to be Bundle at best (alarm clock / kitchen timer / radio). Over-classified at launch is also a failure mode worth naming.

m1-assignment.brief: In one page (cohesive paragraphs, no bullets), pick one technology change you know first-hand and map it to all four scales of disruption. For each scale, name what specifically was replaced (or wasn't — being honest about which scales the case does not reach is half the work). Close by identifying the killer app inside the case.
rubric: Four scales applied — every scale named explicitly
Killer-app identified and defended as the entry point
Honesty about which scales the case does NOT reach
Critical transfer — case is not Edison's bulb
Form · cohesive paragraphs, ≤1 page

paragraphs: The technology change I want to analyse is streaming video. I cancelled my cable subscription in 2018 and have not returned, and I work close enough to a regional broadcaster to see what the disruption looked like from the incumbent's side. The case is unusually clean for the four-scale analysis because it reaches all four scales but did not arrive at them simultaneously.

At the Product layer, the cable set-top box is replaced by a streaming app on a smart TV — same job, different hardware. At the Bundle layer, the cable bundle of live channels + on-demand + DVR is pulled apart strand by strand, with on-demand falling first (around 2010), live sports holding the longest, and live news falling only after the audience itself migrated. At the Industry layer, cable as a content-distribution industry is structurally finished even though the underlying firms survive as internet service providers. The Industry-layer error of the 2010s was reading the cable companies' continued revenue as a sign the disruption was overstated; that revenue was coming from being a pipe, not from being a distributor.

The System layer is where the analysis gets interesting. Streaming did not merely change how people watch television. It dissolved appointment viewing, which dissolved the daily news cycle as a shared cultural artefact, which has measurable consequences for how political information moves. Binge-watching became a default mode of consumption that reshaped the dramatic form itself (prestige TV exists because of streaming, not the other way round). Algorithmic recommendation replaced network programmers as the cultural gatekeeper, with consequences for taste formation that are still being argued about. The killer app inside this case is Netflix on-demand — specifically the original-content gamble around House of Cards in 2013, which justified the subscription model at sufficient scale to attract competitors. Once the subscription was justified, the cascade — original-content arms race, creator economy platforms, live sports moving to streaming, news collapse — rode in on the same infrastructure. The strategic implication for any present-day broadcaster is that the Industry-layer fight is over and the System-layer cascade is well underway; the only question is what cumulative content catalogue and what single strong franchise are worth investing in next.
annotations: Four scales

All four scales named explicitly with concrete artefacts at each. Especially clean on the Industry-layer error — the observation that incumbent firms surviving as ISPs masked the distribution-industry collapse is the move most students miss.
Killer app
Killer app named precisely (Netflix on-demand) and dated (2013, House of Cards). The cascade is concrete and time-ordered.
Honesty about non-reach

Slightly weak here — the essay does not name a scale the case does NOT reach. Streaming does reach all four, so this section had no obvious target, but a paragraph acknowledging which scales the analysis is overconfident on would have lifted the score by half a point.
Critical transfer

The closing turn to the regional-broadcaster strategic question is exactly the move this rubric criterion is asking for. Hold this pattern — Module 1 capstone (Block 5) will ask you to do the same move on humanoid robots, drones, or another live convergence.

m2-overview.body:
Block 2 introduces the first building block of the Seba Technology Disruption Framework: cost curves and the S-curve of adoption. You'll learn why linear forecasts systematically miss the bend, and you'll plot one yourself.

Learning outcomes

  • State the empirical regularity behind technology cost curves and give two historical examples.
  • Read an S-curve correctly — slow-start, acceleration, saturation.
  • Explain why linear extrapolation systematically under-forecasts adoption during acceleration.

m2-lesson-1.videoTitle: Lesson · Cost curves and the S-curve of adoption
m2-lesson-1.body:
Two empirical regularities run through the entire history of technology disruption. They are not theory. They are not metaphors. They are observed patterns that recur, with very few exceptions, across centuries of disruption data. Once you have them in your head, most forecasting failures become legible — including the famous ones.

The first regularity is the cost curve. For most technologies that scale via manufacturing and learning, cost falls by a roughly constant percentage each time cumulative production doubles. The pattern is named after T.P. Wright, who first observed it for airframes in 1936. Solar PV modules: about 20–25% per doubling, sustained since the 1970s. Lithium-ion battery cells: about 18%, sustained since the 1990s. DNA sequencing: faster than Moore's law for a stretch. Over four decades, those percentages compound into orders of magnitude — solar modules are now roughly 500 times cheaper per watt than they were in 1976, and the curve has not bent.

The second regularity is the S-curve of adoption. Cumulative adoption of a new technology traces a sigmoid: slow at the start (the entrant looks like a toy), accelerating sharply through an inflection point (the entrant is suddenly "everywhere"), then decelerating toward saturation as the addressable market is filled. The RethinkX database puts the bulk of adoption — call it 10% to 80% market share — inside roughly a decade for most disruptions in the modern era. Earlier disruptions took longer: landline telephones needed seventy-five years to reach 80% of US households. Smartphones reached billions of users in under a decade. The rate of adoption has been accelerating.

Bradd's figure · the S-curve and the linear trap

Linear forecasts systematically miss the bend

Dotted line — what a linear extrapolation from year-3 data predicts. Solid curve — what actually happens. The gap is what kills incumbents.

The interaction of the two regularities is what makes them dangerous to ignore. The cost curve is doing its work, invisibly, during the slow-start phase, when the technology looks irrelevant. By the time adoption is visible to a casual observer, the cost-parity crossing has usually already happened — and the S-curve has already entered acceleration. The forecaster who waits for evidence of acceleration before revising their model has waited too long. By the inflection point, linear models are wrong by a multiple, and that multiple compounds with every subsequent year.

From Bradd · the IEA's solar track record

Between 2009 and 2022, the International Energy Agency revised its annual solar PV deployment forecast upward in every single one of its World Energy Outlooks. The 2010 outlook projected global installed solar capacity in 2020 at roughly 180 GW. Actual 2020 capacity: about 760 GW — over four times the forecast. The 2014 forecast for 2030: about 600 GW. Actual 2022 capacity already exceeded that target by 30%. The methodology was not malicious. It was structurally linear, applied to a structurally exponential cost curve, on a structurally S-shaped adoption curve. The pattern only changed in 2023, after the team rebuilt the model. The RethinkX team's forecasts, by contrast, were on the high side of the actual outcomes — and almost no one published them.

Once a technology has crossed the cost-parity threshold with its incumbent, the adoption curve usually completes its run inside a decade. That is the conservative end of the empirical range. The aggressive end — smartphones, LEDs, streaming subscriptions — is closer to five years. The number you should keep in your head is not a calendar year; it is the cost-parity date, because once that date passes, the dynamics of the loops we will meet in Block 4 begin to compound on their own.

It is worth saying explicitly why the curves are predictive. Wright's law is not a physical law of nature. It is an empirical regularity rooted in three accumulating effects: economies of scale (fixed costs spread over more units), learning by doing (process refinement that workers cannot articulate but firms capture as productivity), and supply-chain optimisation (component costs falling because the components themselves are on cost curves). The reason the regularity holds across technologies as different as solar PV, lithium-ion, DNA sequencing, and disk storage is that all three effects accumulate in any process that involves manufacturing and iteration. The cases where Wright's law breaks down are mostly cases where one of those three effects is artificially capped — by regulation, by raw-material bottlenecks, or by a process that cannot be iterated quickly.

From Bradd · what STDF got right that the consensus did not

The STDF team's 2017 forecasts for electric-vehicle adoption, solar PV cost, and battery cost were dismissed at the time as wildly aggressive. By 2024 the STDF numbers had been beaten on every metric where the technology was already on its cost curve. McKinsey, the IEA, OPEC, and BloombergNEF were all revising upward toward the STDF range, four to seven years late. The lesson here is not that STDF is magic. It is that any forecaster taking Wright's law seriously and applying an S-curve fit had access to the same conclusion. The information was on the table. The methodology was not.

The "so what?" for the four audiences. Individuals should not buy houses with gas heating in a country where the SWB cost curve is already below grid parity. Firms in an exposed industry should not approve five-year capex plans extrapolated linearly from current demand. Investors should treat any incumbent whose business plan depends on flat adoption of a curve-bound entrant as structurally short, regardless of current cash flows. Policymakers should stop commissioning ten-year studies that take eighteen months to complete and arrive after the inflection has passed. The S-curve is unforgiving to slow institutions.

?
Bradd · mid-lesson check. Name one current technology where you suspect the consensus forecast is linear and the real curve is an S. (Hint: look for any IEA, OPEC, McKinsey or government forecast that has been revised upward three years in a row.) Bonus check: name one technology where you suspect the opposite — the cost curve is hitting a structural floor (raw materials, regulation, physics) and the S-curve will plateau before the consensus expects. Both errors exist; the first is more common.
The cost curve is doing its work while everyone agrees nothing is happening. By the time the news notices, three years of underestimation are already baked into every plan that matters.

Next: the STDF reading, then the mentor session. In the mentor we play the phase-classification game on three live cases — that is the move you will need for the Block 2 plotting exercise.
